quiz.title: Pre-calculus: Unit 2 Quiz 7
quiz.subtitle: 2.7: Piecewise Defined Functions
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Evaluating and understanding functions with multiple rules

questions.question: Given the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x + 1 & \text{if } x < 0 \\ x^2 & \text{if } x \ge 0 \end{cases}$, evaluate $f(-3)$.
questions.explanation: Identify which piece the input belongs to. Since $-3 < 0$, we apply the first rule: $2(-3) + 1 = -6 + 1 = -5$.

questions.question: Using the function $f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x + 1 & \text{if } x < 0 \\ x^2 & \text{if } x \ge 0 \end{cases}$, determine the value of $f(4)$.
questions.explanation: Because $4 \ge 0$, the second rule ($x^2$) is used. Calculating $4^2$ results in $16$.

questions.question: On the graph of a piecewise-defined function, what is indicated by an open circle at a boundary point?
options: The point is included in the graph.
The point is not included in the graph.
questions.explanation: An open circle denotes a boundary that the function approaches but does not reach, typically associated with strict inequalities ($<$ or $>$).

questions.question: Evaluate $g(2)$ for the function $g(x) = \begin{cases} x - 5 & x \le 2 \\ 10 & x > 2 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: The value $x = 2$ satisfies the 'or equal to' condition in the first piece ($x \le 2$). Therefore, $2 - 5 = -3$.

questions.question: Which of these parent functions can be expressed as the piecewise function $f(x) = \begin{cases} x & x \ge 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases}$?
questions.explanation: The absolute value function is defined by its distance from zero, which is $x$ for non-negative numbers and $-x$ for negative numbers.

questions.question: If a piecewise function's graph contains a 'break' where one piece ends and another begins at a different $y$-value, the function is:
questions.explanation: A 'break' or 'jump' in the graph indicates that it is not a single, unbroken curve, which is the definition of a discontinuity.

questions.question: Evaluate $h(0)$ for the function $h(x) = \begin{cases} x + 1 & x < 0 \\ x - 1 & x > 0 \end{cases}$.
questions.explanation: Because neither inequality ($x < 0$ or $x > 0$) includes the value $0$, the function is not defined at that specific point.

questions.question: A data plan costs $\$30$ for up to $2$ GB and $\$10$ for each additional GB. Which expression represents the total cost $C$ for $x$ GB when $x > 2$?
questions.explanation: The base cost is $\$30$. The additional $\$10$ charge applies only to the amount of data used beyond the first 2 GB, represented as $(x - 2)$.

questions.question: When graphing the specific piece $y = 3$ for the domain $x > 5$, you should draw:
options: A horizontal ray with an open circle at $(5, 3)$.
A horizontal line through the origin.
questions.explanation: Since $y$ is constant at 3 for all values greater than 5, the graph is a horizontal ray extending to the right from an open circle at the boundary.

questions.question: Can a piecewise function be classified as an even or odd function?
options: Yes, if its rules and domains satisfy symmetry tests.
No, piecewise functions are always asymmetric.
Only if all pieces are linear.
Only if there is only one piece.
questions.explanation: As long as the composite graph satisfies $f(-x) = f(x)$ or $f(-x) = -f(x)$, the function can be even or odd regardless of being defined in pieces.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can navigate multiple rules with ease.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Pay close attention to which piece contains the 'equal to' sign.
scoreMessages.50: Always check the domain constraint before you plug in your x-value.
scoreMessages.0: Review how to read the 'if' part of a piecewise function.
